Use.title: After the launch, the slow work.
Use.author: Mara Olsen

VStack.Text: The most photographed moment of a release is the launch day.

The work that actually defines whether the release was good — the year that follows — gets almost no coverage. We've been counting hours, in seven companies, in the months after a shipped feature, to understand where they really go.

What we found is consistent enough to call a pattern. The launch is the cheap part. Eighty-two percent of the lifetime engineering effort on a customer-facing feature is spent after the marketing campaign ends — on the quiet work of keeping the thing running, growing, and honest.

Use.quote: We deleted four internal services on the first day. We added six in the next year.

VStack.Text: The shape of that effort is unlike the shape of building it. There are no sprints. The work arrives at irregular intervals, in fragments, often as the consequence of decisions made months earlier. It does not photograph well.

Use.caption: Lifetime engineering hours per feature, normalised. n = 142 features across 7 companies.

Use.label: OF LIFETIME EFFORT
Use.source: Post-launch maintenance, growth iteration, and refactor work. 2024 cohort.

VStack.Text: The numbers come from a private survey of platform-engineering leads we conducted across the second half of 2025. The full methodology, every interview transcript, and the underlying data are in the supplement.

Use.body: Lifetime is bounded at 36 months from GA. Features still in active growth at month 36 are excluded from the median calculation.
